item.title: 午後の挨拶
item.bodyMd:
## お疲れさまです (Otsukaresama desu)

One of the most essential business Japanese phrases. Use it when:
- Greeting colleagues after lunch
- Acknowledging someone's hard work
- As a farewell at the end of the workday

> ⚠️ Don't confuse with ご苦労さま (gokurousama), which is used by superiors to subordinates.
item.explanationText: A polite greeting used in the workplace after noon. Literally means 'you must be tired' but functions as a versatile greeting among colleagues. Used when arriving, leaving, or passing someone at work.

item.title: 今日の天気 — 五月晴れ
item.bodyMd:
## 五月晴れ (Satsuki-bare)

A beautiful compound word:
- 五月 (satsuki) — May (classical Japanese)
- 晴れ (bare/hare) — clear weather

Perfect for describing today's weather in conversation:
「今日は五月晴れですね」(It's beautiful May weather today, isn't it?)
item.explanationText: Clear blue skies typical of May in Japan. Originally referred to clear skies during the rainy season (旧暦五月), now commonly means beautiful May weather. A seasonal word (季語) used in haiku.

item.title: 季節のことば — 薫風
item.bodyMd:
## 薫風 (Kunpū)

- 薫 (kun) — fragrant, aromatic
- 風 (pū/kaze) — wind

Usage example:
「薫風が心地よい季節になりました」
(The season of pleasant fragrant breezes has arrived.)

Often appears in business letters as a seasonal greeting:
「薫風の候、ますますご清栄のこととお喜び申し上げます」
item.explanationText: A fragrant early-summer breeze carrying the scent of fresh green leaves. A refined literary word (文語) often used in formal writing and traditional poetry during May.

item.title: ビジネス表現 — 承知いたしました
item.bodyMd:
## 承知いたしました (Shōchi itashimashita)

Politeness levels of 'understood':
1. 分かった (casual)
2. 分かりました (polite)
3. 了解しました (polite, sometimes seen as informal in strict business)
4. **承知しました** (respectful)
5. **承知いたしました** (most humble/formal)

Use #4 or #5 with clients, superiors, and in email.
item.readingText: しょうちいたしました
item.explanationText: The most polite way to say 'understood/acknowledged' in business Japanese. Combines 承知 (acknowledgment) with いたす (humble form of する). More formal than 分かりました or 了解しました.

item.title: 生活の日本語 — コンビニで
item.bodyMd:
## 温めますか？ (Atatamemasu ka?)

Convenience store essential phrases:
- 「温めますか？」→ Shall I heat this up?
- 「袋いりますか？」→ Do you need a bag?
- 「お箸つけますか？」→ Shall I include chopsticks?
- 「ポイントカードはお持ちですか？」→ Do you have a point card?

Quick replies:
- はい、お願いします (Yes, please)
- 大丈夫です / 結構です (No, thank you)
item.explanationText: You'll hear this at every convenience store when buying a bento or onigiri. The clerk asks if you want your food heated up in the microwave. Reply with はい、お願いします or 大丈夫です.

learningSafeguard.learningObjective: 日本での生活場面を日本語とリスク理解の文脈で学ぶ
learningSafeguard.riskDisclaimer: これは学習目的の内容であり、法律・税務・金融の助言ではありません。必ず公式情報で確認してください。

item.title: 住まいの日本語 — ゴミの分別
item.bodyMd:
## 燃えるゴミ (Moeru gomi) — Burnable Trash

Japan's garbage sorting categories:
- 🔥 燃えるゴミ — burnable (food waste, paper, wood)
- 🧊 燃えないゴミ — non-burnable (ceramics, glass, metals)
- ♻️ 資源ゴミ — recyclables (PET bottles, cans, newspapers)
- 📦 粗大ゴミ — oversized items (furniture, appliances)

⚠️ Collection days vary by neighborhood. Check your local 分別カレンダー (sorting calendar).
item.explanationText: Burnable garbage — one of Japan's main waste categories. Trash sorting (分別) is mandatory. Categories vary by city but typically include: 燃えるゴミ, 燃えないゴミ (non-burnable), 資源ゴミ (recyclables), and 粗大ゴミ (oversized items).

item.title: 銀行の日本語 — ATM操作
item.bodyMd:
## お引き出し (Ohikidashi) — Withdrawal

Japanese ATM screen vocabulary:
- お引き出し — Withdrawal
- お預け入れ — Deposit
- お振り込み — Transfer
- 残高照会 — Balance inquiry
- 通帳記入 — Passbook update
- 暗証番号 — PIN number

💡 Tip: Most convenience store ATMs (セブン銀行, ローソン銀行) support international cards and have English menus.
item.explanationText: Withdrawal — the button you'll see on Japanese ATM screens. Other common ATM terms: お預け入れ (deposit), お振り込み (transfer), 残高照会 (balance inquiry). Many ATMs have English mode available.

item.title: 税金の日本語 — 確定申告
item.bodyMd:
## 確定申告 (Kakutei shinkoku) — Tax Return

Key tax vocabulary:
- 確定申告 — annual tax return
- 源泉徴収 (gensen chōshū) — tax withholding
- 年末調整 (nenmatsu chōsei) — year-end adjustment (for employees)
- 控除 (kōjo) — deduction
- 医療費控除 — medical expense deduction
- e-Tax — online filing system

📅 Filing period: February 16 – March 15 each year.
item.explanationText: Annual tax return filing in Japan. Required for freelancers, those with multiple income sources, or claiming medical expense deductions. Filing period: Feb 16 – Mar 15. Can be done online via e-Tax.
